settings.title: How a Custom Piece Comes Together
settings.excerpt: The drawing you approve is usually the fourth or fifth version, and that is by design.
settings.body:
Walk in for a custom tattoo and you see the final stencil. What you miss is the week of drawing behind it.

After a consultation, our artists go away and sketch. The first idea is rarely the right one. It gets reworked, scaled to your body, tested against how skin actually moves, and reworked again.

From idea to stencil

A custom piece moves through a few clear stages, and each one exists to catch a problem before it reaches your skin. The drawing you eventually approve has usually been through several rounds you never see.

  • A consultation to understand the idea and where it will sit.
  • Rough sketches, most of which get thrown out.
  • Refining the chosen direction and scaling it to your body.
  • A final stencil tested against how the area actually moves.

Reyna, who has been with Inkwell since the start, often redraws a sleeve three times before she is happy. We would rather make you wait a week than rush a thing you will wear for life.

Why the wait is worth it

A tattoo is permanent, and the work that prevents regret all happens in that quiet week of drawing. Rushing it is the one shortcut we will not take.

Your part is simple. Bring references, be honest about what you do and do not like, and trust the artist to handle the placement and flow. The back-and-forth before the needle is exactly what makes the result something you will still be glad of in twenty years.
